judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are strong and comparable on (a), (b), and (c), but Report 1 additionally identifies the shared fixed-path RESULTS_DB collision risk in conftest.py that Report 2 misses, making Report 1 the better overall answer.

report_1.evidence: Group B — `pipeline/registry.py`: F-02, F-05, F-09. Three independent one-line constant edits ... Give all three to one engineer rather than three — there's no parallelism benefit to splitting a file this small, only merge churn.

report_2.evidence: Engineer A — `pipeline/registry.py`: F-02, F-05, F-09. All three edit different constants ... one engineer does all three in one pass.

report_1.evidence: Wave 1 (fully parallel, 4 engineers, Groups A/B/C/D) ... Group B — `pipeline/registry.py`: F-02, F-05, F-09 ... Group C — `pipeline/rubric.py` + new `tests/test_rubric.py`: F-01 ... paired with F-11 (edge-case parsing test)

report_2.evidence: Engineer C — `pipeline/rubric.py`: F-01, then F-11 ... A, B, C, D all start simultaneously in wave 1 ... Engineer A — `pipeline/registry.py`: F-02, F-05, F-09 ... one engineer does all three in one pass.

report_1.evidence: F-06 — underspecified product decision. ... This directly changes every graded answer's output score — exactly the "user-facing behavior change that isn't already a settled decision" project documentation says to escalate rather than let an engineer guess at.

report_2.evidence: F-06 changes `PARTIAL_CREDIT_THRESHOLDS`, which changes the actual numeric grades the pipeline produces for real saved outputs — it's not a pure bug fix ... it needs a concrete target (or a labeled calibration set to tune against) from whoever owns the rubric before it becomes an engineering ticket.

report_1.evidence: `tests/conftest.py` defines `RESULTS_DB` defaulting to a fixed absolute path, `/var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db` ... parallel engineers/CI runs writing to the same absolute path will collide unless each overrides `RESULTS_DB`.
